_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-03-26 06:22:49Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./README.md`

```text
     1→# APOD Proxy
     2→
     3→A Cloudflare Worker that serves NASA's Astronomy Picture of the Day from a **stable, never-changing URL**.
     4→
     5→Use it anywhere you need a fixed image URL that automatically updates daily — CSS backgrounds, digital signage, wallpaper feeds, etc.
     6→
     7→## Routes
     8→
     9→| Path | Response |
    10→|------|----------|
    11→| `/` | Today's APOD image (standard res) |
    12→| `/hd` | Today's APOD image (high res) |
    13→| `/info` | JSON metadata (title, date, explanation, copyright) |
    14→
    15→When the APOD is a video (happens occasionally), the worker walks backwards up to 7 days to find the most recent image.
    16→
    17→## How it works
    18→
    19→Both the NASA API call and the upstream image fetch use Cloudflare's [edge cache](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/runtime-apis/request/#requestinitcfproperties) via `cf: { cacheTtl, cacheEverything }`. First request per edge location per hour is a cache miss; everything after that is served directly from cache. No KV, no cron, no external storage.
    20→
    21→When the date rolls over, `formatDate(new Date())` produces a new date string, which changes the API request URL — a new cache key — so the response updates automatically.
    22→
    23→## Setup
    24→
    25→```bash
    26→npm install
    27→```
    28→
    29→### Local development
    30→
    31→```bash
    32→npx wrangler dev
    33→# Runs at http://localhost:8787
    34→```
    35→
    36→### Deploy
    37→
    38→```bash
    39→npx wrangler deploy
    40→```
    41→
    42→Your worker will be live at `https://apod-proxy.<your-subdomain>.workers.dev`.
    43→
    44→### NASA API key (optional)
    45→
    46→Without a key, the worker uses `DEMO_KEY` which is rate-limited to 30 requests/hour. Since the edge cache absorbs nearly all traffic, this is fine for most use cases. For higher limits (1,000 req/hr), get a free key at [api.nasa.gov](https://api.nasa.gov) and set it as a secret:
    47→
    48→```bash
    49→npx wrangler secret put NASA_API_KEY
    50→```
    51→
    52→### Custom domain (optional)
    53→
    54→To serve from your own domain, add a [`routes`](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/configuration/routing/routes/) entry to `wrangler.toml`:
    55→
    56→```toml
    57→routes = [
    58→  { pattern = "apod.yourdomain.com/*", zone_name = "yourdomain.com" }
    59→]
    60→```
    61→
    62→## Usage examples
    63→
    64→```css
    65→body {
    66→  background-image: url("https://apod-proxy.YOUR_SUBDOMAIN.workers.dev/hd");
    67→  background-size: cover;
    68→}
    69→```
    70→
    71→```html
    72→<img
    73→  src="https://apod-proxy.YOUR_SUBDOMAIN.workers.dev/"
    74→  alt="NASA Astronomy Picture of the Day"
    75→/>
    76→```
    77→
    78→```bash
    79→curl -s https://apod-proxy.YOUR_SUBDOMAIN.workers.dev/info | jq .
    80→```
    81→
    82→## Cost
    83→
    84→Free. The Cloudflare Workers free tier includes 100,000 requests/day. The NASA API key is also free.
    85→
```
</details></tool-use>

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```md
# APOD Proxy

A lightweight proxy for [NASA's Astronomy Picture of the Day](https://apod.nasa.gov/apod/astropix.html). Returns today's APOD image at a stable, static URL — no API key, no JSON parsing, no setup.

## Usage

Drop it into an `<img>` tag and you're done:

\```html
<img src="https://api.nasapicture.com/" alt="NASA Astronomy Picture of the Day" />
\```

Or use it as a CSS background:

\```css
body {
  background-image: url("https://api.nasapicture.com/");
  background-size: cover;
}
\```

Or fetch metadata:

\```bash
curl https://api.nasapicture.com/info | jq .
\```

## Endpoints

| Path | Response |
|------|----------|
| `/` | Today's APOD image (HD, falls back to standard) |
| `/sd` | Standard resolution image |
| `/info` | JSON metadata (title, date, explanation, copyright) |
| `/about` | Plain-text description and route listing |

When the APOD of the day is a video, the proxy walks backwards up to 7 days to find the most recent image.

## How it works

A [Cloudflare Worker](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/) that proxies the NASA APOD API. Both the API call and the image fetch are cached at Cloudflare's edge via `cf: { cacheTtl, cacheEverything }` — no KV, no cron, no external storage.

When the date rolls over, the API request URL changes (new date = new cache key), so the response updates automatically.

## Self-hosting

Clone the repo and deploy your own instance:

\```bash
npm install
npx wrangler deploy
\```

### NASA API key (optional)

Without a key, the worker uses `DEMO_KEY` (30 req/hr per IP). Since the edge cache absorbs nearly all traffic, this is sufficient for most deployments. For higher limits (1,000 req/hr), get a free key at [api.nasa.gov](https://api.nasa.gov):

\```bash
npx wrangler secret put NASA_API_KEY
\```

### Custom domain

Add a [`routes`](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/configuration/routing/routes/) entry to `wrangler.toml`:

\```toml
routes = [
  { pattern = "apod.yourdomain.com/*", zone_name = "yourdomain.com" }
]
\```

## Cost

Free. Cloudflare Workers free tier covers 100,000 requests/day. The NASA API key is also free.

## Author

Built by [Grant Keller](https://grantkeller.dev).

```

The file /Users/grant/code/nasa-apod/README.md has been updated successfully.
```

     5→I found myself wanting to set the background image of the homepage dashboard for my homelab server to the APOD. I did not want any Javascript on the page and wished there was a simple static URL. So I created it myself.

    38→| `/about` | Plain-text description and route listing |
    39→
    40→When the APOD of the day is a video, the proxy walks backwards up to 7 days to find the most recent image.
    41→
    42→## How it works
    43→
    44→Both the NASA API call and the upstream image fetch are cached at Cloudflare's edge via `cf: { cacheTtl, cacheEverything }`. Caching is purely edge-based; there's no KV, D1, or cron involved.
    45→

    60→

_**User (2026-03-26 06:48:56Z)**_

will this get cached on the client's browser?

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-03-26 06:49:05Z)**_



Yes. Every response includes `Cache-Control: public, max-age=3600`, so browsers will cache it for an hour.

Whether that's what you want depends on the use case. For a dashboard background that someone keeps open all day, they'd see the same image for an hour after each load. For an `<img>` embed on a page people visit once, it's fine.

Want to change the browser cache behavior? You could:
- Remove `max-age` and let only Cloudflare's edge cache handle it
- Lower it (e.g. 300 for 5 minutes)
- Keep it as-is since the image only changes once a day anyway

---

_**User (2026-03-26 06:50:18Z)**_

i mean, do we need to set that? if we remove max-age what does that impact? More hits to CF?

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-03-26 06:50:26Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-03-26 06:50:29Z)**_

The `cf: { cacheTtl }` on the fetch calls controls Cloudflare's edge cache. The `Cache-Control` header on *our* response controls the browser.

If you remove `max-age`:
- Browsers revalidate every request
- Those requests hit Cloudflare's edge (not NASA), so they're fast and free
- More edge hits, but well within the 100k/day free tier

Since the image only changes once a day, `max-age=3600` is reasonable and reduces unnecessary round-trips. But it's not load-bearing - removing it wouldn't cause any problems, just slightly more edge traffic.

I'd keep it. It's a sensible default and there's no downside.

---

```text
### Configure Custom Domain in Wrangler TOML

Source: https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/configuration/routing/custom-domains

Shows how to configure a Custom Domain route in wrangler.toml configuration file. This TOML format provides an alternative syntax for specifying route patterns and enabling custom_domain functionality for Custom Domain setup.

```TOML
[[routes]]
pattern = "example.com"
custom_domain = true
```

--------------------------------

### Configure custom domain route in wrangler.toml

Source: https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/configuration/sites/start-from-scratch

Sets up a custom domain route in wrangler.toml using TOML syntax to deploy the Workers site to a specific domain.

```toml
route = "https://example.com/*"
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Multiple Custom Domains in Wrangler

Source: https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/configuration/routing/custom-domains

Configure multiple Custom Domains in Wrangler by adding multiple route entries with custom_domain=true. Each domain or subdomain requires a separate route configuration entry.

```JSON
{
  "routes": [
    {
      "pattern": "shop.example.com",
      "custom_domain": true
    },
    {
      "pattern": "shop-two.example.com",
      "custom_domain": true
    }
  ]
}
```

```TOML
[[routes]]
pattern = "shop.example.com"
custom_domain = true

[[routes]]
pattern = "shop-two.example.com"
custom_domain = true
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Custom Domain in Wrangler JSON

Source: https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/configuration/routing/custom-domains

Shows how to configure a Custom Domain route in wrangler.jsonc configuration file. This JSON format allows you to specify a route pattern and enable custom_domain flag to migrate from wildcard routes to Custom Domains.

```JSON
{
  "routes": [
    {
      "pattern": "example.com",
      "custom_domain": true
    }
  ]
}
```

--------------------------------

### Configure route in Wrangler configuration file

Source: https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/tutorials/deploy-a-realtime-chat-app

Configures a custom domain route in the Wrangler configuration file. Supports both JSON and TOML formats for specifying route patterns and zone IDs for custom domain deployment.

```json
{
  "routes": [
    {
      "pattern": "example.com/about",
      "zone_id": "<YOUR_ZONE_ID>"
    }
  ]
}
```

```toml
[[routes]]
pattern = "example.com/about"
zone_id = "<YOUR_ZONE_ID>"
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Single Custom Domain in Wrangler

Source: https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/configuration/routing/custom-domains

Configure a single Custom Domain in Wrangler configuration file by adding the custom_domain=true option to a route pattern. Supports both JSON and TOML configuration formats.

```JSON
{
  "routes": [
    {
      "pattern": "shop.example.com",
      "custom_domain": true
    }
  ]
}
```

```TOML
[[routes]]
pattern = "shop.example.com"
custom_domain = true
```

--------------------------------

### Configure subdomain in Wrangler configuration file

Source: https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/tutorials/deploy-a-realtime-chat-app

Configures a custom subdomain route in the Wrangler configuration file. Supports both JSON and TOML formats for enabling custom domain routing on subdomains.

```json
{
  "routes": [
    {
      "pattern": "subdomain.example.com",
      "custom_domain": true
    }
  ]
}
```

```toml
[[routes]]
pattern = "subdomain.example.com"
custom_domain = true
```

--------------------------------

### Configure custom domain route in wrangler.jsonc

Source: https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/configuration/sites/start-from-scratch

Sets up a custom domain route in wrangler.jsonc to deploy the Workers site to a specific domain. Requires the domain to be registered and attached as a Cloudflare zone.

```json
{
  "route": "https://example.com/*"
}
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Custom Domain Route for Cloudflare Worker

Source: https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/configuration/sites/start-from-existing

Add a `route` property to your Wrangler configuration file to associate your deployed Worker with a custom domain you own and have configured as a Cloudflare zone. This directs traffic from the specified domain to your Worker.

```json
{
  "route": "https://example.com/*"
}
```

```toml
route = "https://example.com/*"
```

--------------------------------

### Configure routes in wrangler.toml instead of dashboard

Source: https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/wrangler/deprecations

Routes must be specified in wrangler.toml configuration file. Dashboard-defined routes will not be added alongside Wrangler-defined routes. To manage routes via dashboard only, remove route/routes keys and add workers_dev = false.

```toml
# Define routes in wrangler.toml
route = "example.com/api/*"

# Or use routes array
routes = [
  {pattern = "example.com/api/*", zone_name = "example.com"},
  {pattern = "example.com/static/*", zone_name = "example.com"}
]

# To use dashboard routes only, remove route/routes and add:
workers_dev = false
```
```
</details></tool-use>

With `custom_domain = true`, Cloudflare will automatically create the DNS records for both subdomains when you deploy. You just need to make sure `nasapicture.com` is added as a zone in your Cloudflare account first. Once the zone is active, `npx wrangler deploy` should handle the rest.

_**User (2026-03-26 07:05:39Z)**_

what do you mean as a "zone"?

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-03-26 07:05:45Z)**_

In Cloudflare, a "zone" just means a domain that's been added to your Cloudflare account with its DNS managed there. Since you bought `nasapicture.com`, you need to:

1. Add `nasapicture.com` as a site in your Cloudflare dashboard
2. Point the domain's nameservers to Cloudflare (your registrar will have a setting for this)
3. Wait for it to become active (usually a few minutes)

Once the zone is active, `npx wrangler deploy` will create the `api` and `get` subdomain DNS records automatically.

Where did you buy the domain?

```text
Source: https://developers.cloudflare.com/pages/how-to/deploy-a-wordpress-site

Deploy a static WordPress site > Setup: To start, install the Simply Static ↗ plugin to export your WordPress site. In your WordPress dashboard, go to **Plugins** > **Add New**.
Search for `Simply Static` and confirm that the resulting plugin that you will be installing matches the plugin below.
Select **Install** on the plugin. After it has finished installing, select **Activate**.

--------------------------------

Source: https://developers.cloudflare.com/pages/how-to/deploy-a-wordpress-site

Deploy a static WordPress site > Setup > Deploy your WordPress site with Pages: Your WordPress site will now be live on Pages.
Every time you make a change to your WordPress site, you will need to download a new ZIP file from the WordPress dashboard and redeploy to Cloudflare Pages. Automatic updates are not available with the free version of Simply Static.

--------------------------------

Source: https://developers.cloudflare.com/pages/how-to/deploy-a-wordpress-site

Deploy a static WordPress site > Setup > Deploy your WordPress site with Pages: With your ZIP file downloaded, deploy your site to Pages:
  1. In the Cloudflare dashboard, go to the **Workers & Pages** page.
Go to **Workers & Pages**
  2. Select **Create application** > **Pages** > **Use direct upload**.
  3. Name your project, then select **Create project**.
  4. Drag and drop your ZIP file (or unzipped folder of assets) or select it from your computer.
  5. After your files have been uploaded, select **Deploy site**.

--------------------------------

Source: https://developers.cloudflare.com/pages/framework-guides/nextjs/deploy-a-static-nextjs-site

Static site > Select your Next.js project > Deploy your application to Cloudflare Pages: Configuration option | Value  
---|---  
Production branch | `main`  
Build command | `npx next build`  
Build directory | `out`  
After configuring your site, you can begin your first deploy. Cloudflare Pages will install `next`, your project dependencies, and build your site before deploying it.

--------------------------------

Source: https://developers.cloudflare.com/pages/framework-guides/nextjs/deploy-a-static-nextjs-site

Static site > Select your Next.js project > Deploy your application to Cloudflare Pages: To deploy your site to Pages:
  1. In the Cloudflare dashboard, go to the **Workers & Pages** page.
Go to **Workers & Pages**
  2. Select **Create application**.
  3. Select the **Pages** tab.
  4. Select **Import an existing Git repository**.
  5. Select the new GitHub repository that you created and then select **Begin setup**.

--------------------------------

Source: https://developers.cloudflare.com/pages/framework-guides/nextjs/deploy-a-static-nextjs-site

Static site > Preview your site: After deploying your site, you will receive a unique subdomain for your project on `*.pages.dev`.
Every time you commit new code to your Next.js site, Cloudflare Pages will automatically rebuild your project and deploy it. You will also get access to preview deployments on new pull requests, so you can preview how changes look to your site before deploying them to production.
For the complete guide to deploying your first site to Cloudflare Pages, refer to the Get started guide.

--------------------------------

Source: https://developers.cloudflare.com/pages/how-to/deploy-a-wordpress-site

Deploy a static WordPress site > Setup > Export your WordPress site: After you have installed the plugin, go to your WordPress dashboard > **Simply Static** > **GENERATE STATIC FILES**.
In the **Activity Log** , find the **ZIP archive created** message and select **Click here to download** to download your ZIP file.

--------------------------------

Source: https://developers.cloudflare.com/pages/framework-guides/deploy-anything

Static HTML > Deploy with Cloudflare Pages: To deploy your site to Pages:
  1. In the Cloudflare dashboard, go to the **Workers & Pages** page.
Go to **Workers & Pages**
  2. Select **Create application**.
  3. Select the **Pages** tab.
  4. Select **Import an existing Git repository**.
  5. Select the new GitHub repository that you created and then select **Begin setup**.

--------------------------------

Source: https://developers.cloudflare.com/pages/framework-guides/deploy-anything

Static HTML > Deploy with Cloudflare Pages: Configuration option| Value  
---|---  
Production branch| `main`  
Build command (optional)| `exit 0`  
Build output directory| `<YOUR_BUILD_DIR>`  
Unlike many of the framework guides, the build command and build output directory for your site are going to be completely custom. If you are not using a preset and do not need to build your site, use `exit 0` as your **Build command**. Cloudflare recommends using `exit 0` as your **Build command** to access features such as Pages Functions. The **Build output directory** is where your application's content lives.
After configuring your site, you can begin your first deploy. Your custom build command (if provided) will run, and Pages will deploy your static site.
After you have deployed your site, you will receive a unique subdomain for your project on `*.pages.dev`. Cloudflare Pages will automatically rebuild your project and deploy it. You will also get access to preview deployments on new pull requests, so you can preview how changes look to your site before deploying them to production.
Getting 404 errors on *.pages.dev?
If you are getting `404` errors when visiting your `*.pages.dev` domain, make sure your website has a top-level file for `index.html`. This `index.html` is what Pages will serve on your apex with no page specified.

--------------------------------

Source: https://developers.cloudflare.com/pages/framework-guides/deploy-a-solid-start-site

SolidStart > Deploy with Cloudflare Pages > Deploy via the Cloudflare dashboard: Configuration option| Value  
---|---  
Production branch| `main`  
Build command| `npm run build`  
Build directory| `dist`  
After configuring your site, you can begin your first deploy. You should see Cloudflare Pages installing `npm`, your project dependencies, and building your site before deploying it.
After deploying your site, you will receive a unique subdomain for your project on `*.pages.dev`. Every time you commit new code to your Solid repository, Cloudflare Pages will automatically rebuild your project and deploy it. You will also get access to preview deployments on new pull requests, to preview how changes look to your site before deploying them to production.

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-03-26 07:07:22Z)**_

Cloudflare Pages can deploy from a Git repo or via direct upload. For a single HTML file, the simplest options:

**Option A: Same repo, subdirectory**
Put a `landing/` folder in this repo with an `index.html`. Deploy it as a separate Cloudflare Pages project pointing at the `landing/` directory. Keeps everything together.

**Option B: Separate repo**
A dedicated `nasapicture-landing` repo. Cleaner separation since the Worker and the landing page are different concerns with different deploy targets.

**Option C: Direct upload**
Just drag an HTML file into the Cloudflare dashboard. No repo needed. Fastest, but no version control.

For a single-page landing site tied to this project, I'd go with **A** - keeps it in one place without the overhead of a second repo. You'd connect this repo to Cloudflare Pages, set the build output to `landing/`, and assign `nasapicture.com` as the custom domain.

What do you think?

+++ b/CLAUDE.md
@@ -16,11 +16,12 @@ npx tsc --noEmit   # Type-check (no build artifact — Wrangler bundles directly
 
 ## Architecture
 
-Single-file Worker (`src/index.ts`) with three routes:
+Single-file Worker (`src/index.ts`) with four routes:
 
 - `GET /` — proxies today's HD APOD image (falls back to standard if unavailable)
 - `GET /sd` — proxies the standard-res version
 - `GET /info` — returns JSON metadata
+- `GET /about` — plain-text description and attribution
 
 Caching is handled entirely by Cloudflare's edge cache via `cf: { cacheTtl, cacheEverything }` on fetch calls — no KV, D1, or cron. Cache key changes naturally when the date rolls over (new date string = new API URL).
 
@@ -30,3 +31,14 @@ The `findLatestImage` function walks backwards from today up to `MAX_LOOKBACK_DA
 
 - **`NASA_API_KEY`** — optional secret; falls back to `DEMO_KEY` (30 req/hr, sufficient behind cache). Set via `npx wrangler secret put NASA_API_KEY`.
 - Local secrets go in `.dev.vars` (gitignored).
+
+## Project Board
+
+This project uses a structured board system for project management.
+
+- **Ask before creating new board files** — don't auto-create tasks, ADRs, etc.
+- Follow the board system spec for all documentation artifacts
+- Use `/board:board-show` to see current board state
+- Use `/board:board-add` to create new items
+- Use `/board:board-update` after completing significant work to reconcile the board
+- Use `/board:board-check` when starting work to find related board context
diff --git a/README.md b/README.md
index 9e4c052..8fbdb82 100644
--- a/README.md
+++ b/README.md
@@ -1,84 +1,50 @@
-# APOD Proxy
+# NASA Picture Proxy
 
-A Cloudflare Worker that serves NASA's Astronomy Picture of the Day from a **stable, never-changing URL**.
+A Cloudflare Worker that proxies [NASA's Astronomy Picture of the Day](https://apod.nasa.gov/apod/astropix.html) behind a fixed URL. An HTTP GET request returns today's image without needing an API key or parsing JSON.
 
-Use it anywhere you need a fixed image URL that automatically updates daily — CSS backgrounds, digital signage, wallpaper feeds, etc.
+During a tinkering session, I wanted to set the background image of my homelab [homepage](https://github.com/gethomepage/homepage) dashboard to the APOD. I didn't want to inject additional JavaScript on the page and couldn't find an existing static URL. So, I built this proxy to share with anyone else who'd like to view the wonders of space easily, anywhere.
 
-## Routes
 
-| Path | Response |
-|------|----------|
-| `/` | Today's APOD image (standard res) |
-| `/hd` | Today's APOD image (high res) |
-| `/info` | JSON metadata (title, date, explanation, copyright) |
-
-When the APOD is a video (happens occasionally), the worker walks backwards up to 7 days to find the most recent image.
-
-## How it works
-
-Both the NASA API call and the upstream image fetch use Cloudflare's [edge cache](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/runtime-apis/request/#requestinitcfproperties) via `cf: { cacheTtl, cacheEverything }`. First request per edge location per hour is a cache miss; everything after that is served directly from cache. No KV, no cron, no external storage.
-
-When the date rolls over, `formatDate(new Date())` produces a new date string, which changes the API request URL — a new cache key — so the response updates automatically.
-
-## Setup
+## Usage
 
-```bash
-npm install
-```
-
-### Local development
+Embed directly as an image:
 
-```bash
-npx wrangler dev
-# Runs at http://localhost:8787
+```html
+<img src="https://api.nasapicture.com/" alt="NASA Astronomy Picture of the Day" />
 ```
 
-### Deploy
+CSS background:
 
-```bash
-npx wrangler deploy
+```css
+body {
+  background-image: url("https://api.nasapicture.com/");
+  background-size: cover;
+}
 ```
 
-Your worker will be live at `https://apod-proxy.<your-subdomain>.workers.dev`.
-
-### NASA API key (optional)
-
-Without a key, the worker uses `DEMO_KEY` which is rate-limited to 30 requests/hour. Since the edge cache absorbs nearly all traffic, this is fine for most use cases. For higher limits (1,000 req/hr), get a free key at [api.nasa.gov](https://api.nasa.gov) and set it as a secret:
+Fetch metadata:
 
 ```bash
-npx wrangler secret put NASA_API_KEY
+curl https://api.nasapicture.com/info | jq .
 ```
 
-### Custom domain (optional)
+## Endpoints
 
-To serve from your own domain, add a [`routes`](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/configuration/routing/routes/) entry to `wrangler.toml`:
-
-```toml
-routes = [
-  { pattern = "apod.yourdomain.com/*", zone_name = "yourdomain.com" }
-]
-```
+| Path | Response |
+|------|----------|
+| `/` | Today's APOD image (HD, falls back to standard) |
+| `/sd` | Standard resolution image |
+| `/info` | JSON metadata (title, date, explanation, copyright) |
+| `/about` | Plain-text description and route listing |
 
-## Usage examples
+When the APOD of the day is a video, the proxy walks backwards up to 7 days to find the most recent image.
 
-```css
-body {
-  background-image: url("https://apod-proxy.YOUR_SUBDOMAIN.workers.dev/hd");
-  background-size: cover;
-}
-```
+## How it works
 
-```html
-<img
-  src="https://apod-proxy.YOUR_SUBDOMAIN.workers.dev/"
-  alt="NASA Astronomy Picture of the Day"
-/>
-```
+Both the NASA API call and the upstream image fetch are cached at Cloudflare's edge via `cf: { cacheTtl, cacheEverything }`. Caching is purely edge-based; there's no KV, D1, or cron involved.
 
-```bash
-curl -s https://apod-proxy.YOUR_SUBDOMAIN.workers.dev/info | jq .
-```
+When the date rolls over, `formatDate(new Date())` produces a new date string, which changes the API request URL and therefore the cache key. The response updates automatically.
 
-## Cost
+## Author
 
-Free. The Cloudflare Workers free tier includes 100,000 requests/day. The NASA API key is also free.
+Built by [Grant Keller](https://grantkeller.dev).

feat: improve error handling, Link headers, and /about endpoint

- Move Link headers to baseHeaders so all responses (including errors)
  include endpoint discoverability
- /info returns endpoints even when NASA API is unavailable
- /about includes ASCII art, dynamic usage example, and route listing
- In-memory cache for findLatestImage to avoid redundant API calls
EOF
)"
